posts.title: agents aren't the product, the workflow is
posts.hook: Everyone's shipping AI agents. Almost nobody is shipping AI workflows.
posts.body:
Everyone's shipping AI agents. Almost nobody is shipping AI workflows.

I've been building with Claude Code for months now. Not as a toy. As the core of a production system that runs crons, generates content, manages deployments, and orchestrates multi-step GTM pipelines.

The agent itself is maybe 10% of the value. The other 90% is the workflow wrapping it. Error handling. State persistence. Retry logic. Context handoffs between sessions. Knowing when to stop and ask vs. when to just ship.

Most agent demos show a single prompt doing a single thing. Cool. Now make it run at midnight every night, recover from failures, write to a database, and not hallucinate your client's name into a public repo.

That's where the engineering lives.

I have a daily cron that scans git history, scores output, generates a dashboard card, and posts a blog. Zero human intervention. It breaks sometimes. The fix is never "better prompting." It's better infrastructure. Durable steps. Idempotent writes. Graceful degradation.

If you're building agents without thinking about the workflow lifecycle around them, you're building demos, not systems.
posts.cta: What's the ugliest workflow problem you've hit with agents in production?

inspired_by.author: Swyx
inspired_by.summary: Swyx discusses the evolution of AI agents, highlighting how multi-agent systems are transforming workflows beyond simple automation. He shares insights on integrating developer tools for scalable AI infrastructure. Emphasizes practical challenges in deployment and real-world case studies.

remixes.label: infrastructure-first
remixes.hook: Your AI agent is only as good as the plumbing it runs on.
remixes.body:
Your AI agent is only as good as the plumbing it runs on.

I watch teams demo beautiful agent interactions. Smart responses. Tool calling. Multi-step reasoning. Then I ask: what happens when the API times out at 3am? What happens when the model returns malformed JSON and your downstream system chokes?

Silence.

I've been running Claude Code as production infrastructure for months. The agent logic took a weekend. The surrounding system took weeks. State machines for multi-step pipelines. Graceful degradation when rate limits hit. Idempotent retries that don't duplicate work. Logging that actually tells you what went wrong instead of just "error."

The unglamorous truth: the agent is a function call. The workflow is the product. Error handling is the product. Observability is the product.

Most agent demos skip the part where real systems break. Real systems break constantly. The question isn't whether your agent can complete a task in ideal conditions. It's whether your workflow can recover when conditions aren't ideal.

Stop building smarter agents. Start building more resilient workflows.
remixes.cta: What percentage of your agent project is actually agent logic vs. infrastructure?

remixes.label: the-boring-90-percent
remixes.hook: I spent a weekend building an AI agent. I've spent months building the system that keeps it alive.
remixes.body:
I spent a weekend building an AI agent. I've spent months building the system that keeps it alive.

Here's what nobody posts about on LinkedIn. The retry logic. The state persistence between steps. The cron job that fires at midnight and needs to handle three different failure modes gracefully. The diff between what Claude returns and what your database schema actually accepts.

This is the real work of AI in production.

My system runs content generation, deployment orchestration, and multi-step GTM pipelines. The Claude Code integration is elegant. The error handling around it is ugly, battle-tested, and the only reason it actually works.

I've learned that agent reliability follows a power law. The first 80% of reliability is easy. The next 15% is hard. The last 5% is where you spend most of your time. And that last 5% is entirely about workflow design, not model capability.

The teams winning with AI agents right now aren't the ones with the best prompts. They're the ones who treated the surrounding workflow like a real engineering problem.
remixes.cta: What's the most time you've spent on a single error-handling edge case in an AI system?

Everyone's shipping AI agents. Almost nobody is shipping AI workflows.

Here's what I'd recommend if you're building agents for production: spend 20% of your time on the agent logic and 80% on the workflow wrapping it. I've built these systems across content pipelines, GTM automation, and deployment orchestration. The pattern is consistent.

The agent is a capability. The workflow is the product. That means error handling with real fallback paths, not just try/catch. State persistence so a failed step doesn't restart the entire pipeline. Retry logic with exponential backoff and budget caps so you don't burn API credits on a loop.

I run Claude Code as production infrastructure. Crons fire nightly. Content gets generated, validated, and deployed. The agent handles maybe 10% of the complexity. The orchestration layer handles the rest.

My recommendation: before you build another agent feature, audit your failure modes. Map every point where the model output touches your system. Build recovery paths for each one. That's where production reliability lives.

The teams I see succeeding treat their agent workflows like distributed systems, because that's what they are.

And honestly? Most agent demos are vaporware with a chatbot UI.

Here's the uncomfortable part. Your agent that works perfectly in a demo will fall apart in production within 72 hours. Not because the model is bad. Because you didn't build the boring stuff. You skipped error handling because it wasn't fun. You skipped state persistence because "we'll add that later." You skipped retry logic because the demo worked on the first try.

I've been running Claude Code as real infrastructure for months. The agent is maybe 10% of the value. The other 90% is the ugly plumbing that keeps it running at 2am when nobody's watching.

The agent hype cycle has a dirty secret: the people actually shipping AI in production aren't talking about agents. They're talking about workflows, state machines, and error budgets. The same boring infrastructure concepts we've had for decades.

Stop fetishizing the agent. Start respecting the workflow. The model is a commodity. The orchestration is the moat.

posts.title: outbound automation backlash is correct
posts.hook: The backlash against outbound automation isn't anti-tech. It's anti-lazy.
posts.body:
The backlash against outbound automation isn't anti-tech. It's anti-lazy.

I used to send 200+ cold emails a day from primary domains with zero warmup on SalesLoft. I know what volume-first outbound looks like from the inside. It works until it doesn't. Then your domain is toast and your pipeline evaporates overnight.

The tools got better. The thinking didn't.

Teams are plugging Clay into Instantly into Smartlead and blasting sequences that feel personalized but aren't. The recipient can tell. Open rates look fine. Reply rates are in the gutter. Meetings booked per 1000 sends are worse than 2019.

The fix isn't "go back to manual." The fix is using automation for the research layer, not the messaging layer. Let Clay enrich, score, and cluster. Let a human (or a very well-prompted model with real context) write the actual message.

I build automated pipelines for a living and I'm telling you... the ones that perform have a human checkpoint before send. Every time.

Automation should make your outreach smarter, not just faster.
posts.cta: Where do you draw the line between automated research and automated messaging?

inspired_by.author: Josh Braun
inspired_by.summary: Josh critiques overhyped outbound automation tools and advocates for personalized, human-led outreach at scale. He provides tips on using AI ethically for prospecting without spamming inboxes. Shares case studies of 3x reply rates with low-volume, high-quality sequences.

remixes.label: volume-is-a-trap
remixes.hook: I burned a primary domain sending 200 cold emails a day. Here's what I wish someone had told me.
remixes.body:
I burned a primary domain sending 200 cold emails a day. Here's what I wish someone had told me.

Back when I ran outbound on SalesLoft, the playbook was simple: more volume, more pipeline. It worked for about three months. Then deliverability cratered. The domain got flagged. Pipeline didn't slow down gradually. It disappeared overnight.

Fast forward to now. The tools are infinitely better. Clay, Instantly, Apollo, AI-generated personalization at scale. But the core mistake hasn't changed. Teams are still optimizing for volume when they should be optimizing for signal.

The best outbound I've seen recently doesn't look like outbound at all. It looks like a well-timed, relevant message that arrives right when someone is actively evaluating. That requires research infrastructure, not send infrastructure.

Automate the research. Automate the enrichment. Automate the timing signals. But the moment you automate the actual message without human judgment on whether it should be sent at all, you're back in the volume trap.

The line isn't between manual and automated. It's between thoughtful and lazy.
remixes.cta: Have you ever had to rebuild a domain reputation from scratch? How long did it take?

remixes.label: research-vs-reach
remixes.hook: The outbound tools aren't the problem. The strategy loading them is.
remixes.body:
The outbound tools aren't the problem. The strategy loading them is.

Clay is incredible for research. Apollo has solid data. Instantly handles deliverability well. None of these tools are bad. But stack them together without a clear targeting thesis and you get expensive spam with better grammar.

I've built outbound systems both ways. The volume way: scrape a list, enrich it, generate personalized emails, blast. The signal way: identify buying triggers, research the account, craft a message that references something real, send when the timing makes sense.

The volume way scales faster. The signal way actually works.

The backlash against outbound automation is really backlash against the volume approach dressed up in better tooling. People aren't mad at technology. They're mad at receiving obviously templated messages that pretend to be personal.

The fix isn't less automation. It's automating the right things. Automate research, enrichment, and trigger detection. Keep human judgment on message quality and send decisions.

The teams doing outbound well right now are spending more on research infrastructure than send infrastructure. That's the shift.
remixes.cta: What's your ratio of time spent on research vs. time spent on messaging in your outbound process?

The backlash against outbound automation isn't anti-tech. It's anti-lazy.

I've been on both sides of this. I ran high-volume outbound from primary domains with zero warmup. I've also built signal-based systems that send a fraction of the volume and generate better pipeline. Here's what I recommend based on that experience.

First, separate your research automation from your messaging automation. These are different problems. Automating research, enrichment, and buying signal detection is almost always a good investment. Automating the actual send decision without human review is where things break.

Second, audit your personalization. If your "personalized" emails reference something anyone could find in 10 seconds on a LinkedIn profile, that's not personalization. That's a template with a variable. Real personalization demonstrates you understand the prospect's specific situation.

Third, protect your domain infrastructure. Use separate sending domains. Warm them properly. Monitor deliverability daily, not monthly. One bad week can undo months of reputation building.

The tools available now are genuinely powerful. Clay, Apollo, Instantly. But they amplify your strategy, good or bad. If your targeting is lazy, better tools just help you be lazy faster.

The backlash against outbound automation isn't anti-tech. It's anti-lazy.

And let's be real. Most of you reading this are part of the problem.

You plugged Clay into Instantly, pointed it at a scraped list, and called it a "personalized outbound motion." Your emails start with "I noticed you" followed by something scraped from a LinkedIn headline. That's not personalization. That's a mail merge with extra steps.

I used to send 200+ cold emails a day. I know exactly what this looks like from the inside. It feels productive. The dashboards show activity. Your manager sees volume. And then one morning your domain reputation score drops to zero and your pipeline vanishes like it was never there.

The tools got better. The thinking got lazier.

Here's the part nobody wants to hear: if your outbound strategy requires sending hundreds of messages to work, your targeting is broken. Period. Better tools won't fix broken targeting. They'll just help you burn through your addressable market faster.

The teams actually winning at outbound right now send fewer messages, not more. They spend more time on research than on copy. And they never, ever automate the send decision.

posts.title: solo builders have an unfair advantage right now
posts.hook: A solo builder with Claude Code, Vercel, and a clear problem to solve ships faster than a 5-person team with a project manager.
posts.body:
A solo builder with Claude Code, Vercel, and a clear problem to solve ships faster than a 5-person team with a project manager.

This isn't hypothetical. I run a monorepo with three Next.js sites, a mission control app, automated content pipelines, CRM integrations, and a daily cron system. One person. One Mac Mini.

The leverage isn't "AI writes my code." The leverage is AI handles the 40% of work that used to require context switching. Boilerplate. Config. Debugging that third-party API response format. Writing the test I know I need but don't want to write.

I still architect everything. I still make every product decision. I still review every deploy. But the gap between "I want this" and "this is live" collapsed from days to hours.

The builders who figure this out first aren't waiting for permission. They're shipping. Pieter Levels has been proving this model for years. The difference now is the tools caught up to the ambition.

You don't need a team to build something real. You need taste, a workflow, and the discipline to ship before it's perfect.
posts.cta: What's the most complex thing you've shipped solo this year?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey as a solo developer building and shipping multiple successful products like Nomad List without VC funding. He emphasizes the power of building in public, iterating quickly based on user feedback, and achieving profitability through organic growth. His post details revenue figures and lessons for other indie makers.

remixes.label: leverage-redefined
remixes.hook: The gap between a solo builder and a small team just flipped. And most teams haven't noticed.
remixes.body:
The gap between a solo builder and a small team just flipped. And most teams haven't noticed.

Small teams used to have the advantage. Division of labor. Specialization. One person handles frontend, another handles backend, a PM keeps everything on track. That math made sense when building was the bottleneck.

Building isn't the bottleneck anymore. Decision-making is.

A solo builder with Claude Code makes one decision and immediately executes. No standup. No ticket. No PR review queue. No "let's sync on this tomorrow." The feedback loop between idea and implementation is measured in minutes, not sprints.

I run three Next.js sites, a mission control dashboard, automated content pipelines, CRM integrations, and a daily cron system. One person. One Mac Mini. The AI doesn't write my code for me. It handles the 40% of work that used to require context switching, boilerplate, and administrative overhead.

This isn't about replacing teams. It's about recognizing that for certain types of work, coordination cost exceeds the benefit of specialization. And AI just lowered the value of specialization while leaving coordination cost the same.

The solo builders who see this clearly are shipping at a pace that looks impossible from inside a team.
remixes.cta: What's one task you still think requires a team that a solo builder could actually handle now?

remixes.label: coordination-tax
remixes.hook: Every meeting, standup, and async thread is a tax on shipping speed. Solo builders don't pay it.
remixes.body:
Every meeting, standup, and async thread is a tax on shipping speed. Solo builders don't pay it.

I'm not anti-team. I've built and led teams. But there's a math problem that most organizations ignore. Coordination cost scales non-linearly with team size. Two people need one communication channel. Five people need ten. Ten people need forty-five.

Now add AI to the equation. A solo builder with Claude Code, Vercel, and modern tooling can handle the workload that used to justify a small team. Not because AI is magic. Because AI eliminates the work that used to require extra people. Boilerplate. Research. Context switching between codebases. Deployment automation.

My stack: monorepo with three production sites, automated content system, CRM integrations, cron pipelines. Zero coordination cost. Every decision is made and executed in the same session.

The unfair advantage isn't talent. Plenty of engineers in teams are more talented than me. The advantage is zero latency between decision and execution. No waiting for approvals. No explaining context to someone who wasn't in the last meeting.

This window won't last forever. Teams will adapt. Coordination tools will improve. But right now, solo builders with the right stack have a speed advantage that's genuinely hard to compete with.
remixes.cta: What's the biggest coordination tax you pay on your current team?

And the 5-person team knows it. They just can't admit it because their org chart depends on the old math.

I run three production sites, automated pipelines, CRM integrations, and a daily cron system. One person. One Mac Mini. No standups. No sprint planning. No Jira tickets describing work that takes longer to document than to do.

: most small teams exist because building used to be hard enough to require specialization. It's not anymore. AI didn't replace the engineers. It replaced the justification for having five of them on the same project.

The 40% of work that used to require extra people, the boilerplate, the research, the context switching, AI handles that now. What's left is decisions and execution. One person with clear thinking handles both.

I know this is spicy. I know people will say "but what about code review" and "but what about knowledge sharing." Valid concerns for large systems. But for most products at most startups? The coordination cost of a 5-person team is the biggest drag on shipping speed. Not the code.

Solo builders have an unfair advantage right now. It won't last forever. But it's real.

posts.title: GTM positioning is broken at most startups
posts.hook: Most SaaS companies position their product by listing features. Their competitors list the same features. Then everyone wonders why pipeline is slow.
posts.body:
Most SaaS companies position their product by listing features. Their competitors list the same features. Then everyone wonders why pipeline is slow.

I've built GTM systems for enough companies to see the pattern. The ones that close deals fast have one thing in common: they can explain who they're for and why in under 10 seconds. Not what they do. Who it's for and why it matters to that specific person.

The ones struggling? They lead with "AI-powered platform for..." and lose the buyer before the fold.

Positioning isn't a marketing exercise. It's a pipeline exercise. When your SDRs can't articulate differentiation in a cold email, that's a positioning failure. When your landing page converts at 1.2%, that's a positioning failure. When deals stall at demo because the prospect says "this looks like X competitor," that's a positioning failure.

I build automated enrichment pipelines that pull technographic and intent signals. But the best signal in the world doesn't help if your message sounds like everyone else's message.

Fix the positioning before you scale the pipeline. Otherwise you're just automating mediocrity faster.
posts.cta: When's the last time you rewrote your ICP definition from scratch instead of tweaking it?

inspired_by.author: April Dunford
inspired_by.summary: April dives into GTM positioning strategies for SaaS, stressing product-led narratives over feature dumps. She advises on outbound messaging frameworks that resonate with buyer pain points. Ties it to content strategy for thought leadership amplification.

remixes.label: the-10-second-test
remixes.hook: I ask every founder the same question: who is your product for and why should they care? Most can't answer in under 60 seconds.
remixes.body:
I ask every founder the same question: who is your product for and why should they care? Most can't answer in under 60 seconds.

That's the whole problem.

If your team can't articulate your positioning in 10 seconds, your prospects definitely can't. And if prospects can't explain why you're different, they default to comparing features. Feature comparisons always end in a race to the bottom.

I've built GTM systems for enough companies to see this pattern repeat. The companies that close deals fast don't have better products. They have clearer positioning. They can tell you exactly who they serve and exactly why it matters to that specific person.

The companies with slow pipeline almost always have the same disease: positioning by feature list. "We do X, Y, and Z." Cool. So do your three closest competitors. Now what?

Positioning isn't a messaging exercise. It's a strategic decision about who you're willing to exclude. If your ICP is "any company with 50+ employees that uses software," you don't have an ICP. You have a prayer.

The fix is uncomfortable: narrow your target until it feels too small. Then build your entire message around that specific person's specific problem. Everything else, the features, the capabilities, the platform story, comes after.

Clarity converts. Vagueness doesn't.
remixes.cta: Can your sales team explain your positioning in 10 seconds without mentioning a feature?

remixes.label: icp-autopsy
remixes.hook: Your ICP doc is probably a list of firmographics. That's not positioning. That's a filter.
remixes.body:
Your ICP doc is probably a list of firmographics. That's not positioning. That's a filter.

"Series A to C SaaS companies with 50-500 employees" tells you nothing about why someone would buy your product. It tells you who might show up in a search result. These are different things.

I've reviewed dozens of ICP documents while building GTM systems. The pattern is always the same. Demographics and firmographics are well-defined. Psychographics and pain triggers are vague or missing entirely.

The companies with fast pipeline define their ICP around a problem state, not a company profile. Not "mid-market SaaS companies" but "engineering teams that just hit the wall where manual QA can't keep up with release velocity and they're starting to miss bugs in production."

That's a person you can write a message to. That's a situation you can identify with buying signals. That's positioning that excludes enough people to actually mean something.

Most teams tweak their ICP quarterly. Small adjustments. Shift the employee count range. Add an industry vertical. This is deck chair rearrangement.

The real move is burning your ICP doc and rebuilding it around a moment, not a market. What is the specific situation someone is in when your product becomes the obvious answer? Start there. Build everything else around that moment.
remixes.cta: Does your ICP describe a company profile or a problem state? Honest answers only.

The ones struggling? They lead with "AI-powered platform for..." and lose the buyer before the fold.

Positioning isn't a marketing exercise. It's a pipeline exercise. When your SDRs can't articulate differentiation in a cold email, that's a positioning failure. When your landing page converts at 1.2%, that's a positioning failure. When deals stall at demo because the prospect says "this looks like X competitor," that's a positioning failure.

I build automated enrichment pipelines that pull technographic and intent signals. But the best signal in the world doesn't help if your message sounds like everyone else's message.

Fix the positioning before you scale the pipeline. Otherwise you're just automating mediocrity faster.

Second, rebuild your ICP around a problem state instead of firmographics. "Series B SaaS companies" is a filter, not positioning. "Engineering leads who just realized their manual QA process can't keep up with their release cadence" is positioning. One tells you who to target. The other tells you what to say.

Finally, align your entire funnel around the positioning. Website copy, outbound messaging, sales deck, objection handling. If any of these tell a different story, you're creating friction in your own pipeline.

posts.title: the real AI infrastructure problem
posts.hook: The hardest part of running AI in production isn't the model. It's everything around the model.
posts.body:
The hardest part of running AI in production isn't the model. It's everything around the model.

Context management. Token budgets. Rate limits at 2am when your cron fires. Handling the diff between what the model returns and what your database expects. Retry logic that doesn't burn your API credits on the same failed request 47 times.

I run multiple AI models in production daily. Claude Opus for content generation. Sonnet for chat. Qwen locally for high-frequency tasks where I can't justify API costs. Each one has different failure modes.

The local Ollama instance is free but slow and occasionally garbles structured output. The API models are reliable but expensive if you're not careful about context window management. And every single one of them will confidently return malformed JSON at least once a week.

Nobody talks about this layer because it's not sexy. There's no demo for "I wrote a retry wrapper that exponentially backs off and falls back to a cheaper model when the primary is rate-limited."

But that's the infrastructure that separates "I built a cool thing" from "I built a thing that runs every day without me touching it."

The model is the easy part. The plumbing is the job.
posts.cta: What's your ugliest AI-in-production hack that actually works?

remixes.label: the-2am-cron-problem
remixes.hook: My cron job fired at 2am, hit the rate limit, retried 47 times, and burned $12 in API credits before I woke up. Welcome to AI infrastructure.
remixes.body:
My cron job fired at 2am, hit the rate limit, retried 47 times, and burned $12 in API credits before I woke up. Welcome to AI infrastructure.

Nobody talks about this part. The glamorous side of AI in production is the demo. The model responding intelligently. The automation completing a complex task. The pipeline generating content that actually sounds good.

The unglamorous side is debugging why your token budget calculation was off by 2x because the model's response included a JSON block with escaped newlines that your tokenizer counted differently than the API.

I run multiple AI models in production daily. Claude Opus for content creation. Sonnet for the chat interface. Qwen locally for high-frequency tasks that would bankrupt me on API calls. Each one has different failure modes. Different rate limits. Different output formats that need different parsing.

The real infrastructure problems: context management across multi-step pipelines. State persistence when a step fails midway. Cost tracking granular enough to know which pipeline is burning budget. Monitoring that pages you before the damage is done, not after.

None of this is model innovation. It's operations. And it's where most AI projects actually die. Not because the model wasn't smart enough. Because the infrastructure wasn't resilient enough.
remixes.cta: What's the most expensive AI infrastructure mistake you've caught before it spiraled?

remixes.label: multi-model-ops
remixes.hook: Running one AI model in production is straightforward. Running four is an operations problem nobody prepared you for.
remixes.body:
Running one AI model in production is straightforward. Running four is an operations problem nobody prepared you for.

I run Claude Opus for long-form content. Sonnet for interactive chat. Qwen 2.5 locally on Ollama for high-frequency crons that would cost a fortune on API calls. Each model has different strengths, different failure modes, and different cost profiles.

This is the part that doesn't make it into the AI hype cycle. Model selection per task. Token budget management across providers. Rate limit handling that's different for every API. Output parsing that accounts for each model's formatting quirks.

A few things I've learned the hard way. First, your retry logic needs per-provider configuration. Anthropic rate limits are different from OpenAI rate limits are different from local model timeouts. Generic retry logic will either be too aggressive or too conservative for at least one of them.

Second, cost tracking per pipeline is essential. I know exactly which cron costs what per day. Without that granularity, you'll blow budget on a pipeline that ran fine for weeks and then hit an edge case that 10x'd your token usage.

Third, local models aren't free. They're free in API costs. They cost you in maintenance, memory management, and the overhead of keeping Ollama running reliably.

The real AI infrastructure challenge isn't making models work. It's making the system around them work reliably, affordably, and at 2am without you.
remixes.cta: How many different models are you running in production and how do you manage the differences?

The hardest part of running AI in production isn't the model. It's everything around the model.

Here's what I recommend focusing on if you're moving from AI prototypes to production systems.

Context management should be your first investment. Multi-step pipelines need to carry state between steps without exceeding token limits. Build a context budget system early. Know exactly how many tokens each step consumes and set hard caps.

Retry logic needs to be provider-aware. Anthropic, OpenAI, and local models all have different rate limit patterns. Build retry configurations per provider with exponential backoff and budget caps. A generic retry wrapper will either burn credits or give up too early.

Cost tracking needs pipeline-level granularity. Knowing your total monthly AI spend is table stakes. Knowing which specific pipeline or cron job is responsible for which portion of that spend is what actually lets you optimize.

Monitoring should catch failures before they compound. A cron that fails silently at 2am and retries aggressively can burn significant budget before anyone notices. Set up cost alerts per pipeline, not just account-wide.

Finally, consider a multi-model strategy. Not every task needs your most capable model. I run Opus for content creation, Sonnet for chat, and Qwen locally for high-frequency work. Match model capability to task requirements and cost sensitivity.

The model is a commodity. The operational layer around it is your actual competitive advantage.

posts.title: revenue operations ate sales ops
posts.hook: Sales ops is dead. It just doesn't know it yet.
posts.body:
Sales ops is dead. It just doesn't know it yet.

Every company I work with that still has a standalone "sales ops" function is fighting the same battle: they own Salesforce, they build reports, they manage territories, and they have zero visibility into what marketing is doing upstream or what CS is doing downstream.

RevOps isn't just a rebrand. It's a structural shift. The companies winning right now have a single team that owns the data model from first touch to renewal. One attribution framework. One enrichment pipeline. One source of truth.

I build these systems. The technical work is straightforward. Attio or HubSpot as the CRM, Clay for enrichment, PostHog for product analytics, a scoring model that weights intent signals across the full journey.

The hard part is never the tools. It's getting sales, marketing, and CS to agree on definitions. What's an MQL? What's a qualified opportunity? When does a lead become pipeline? Three teams, three answers, three dashboards showing different numbers.

The RevOps role exists because somebody has to own that reconciliation. If your sales ops team is still just building Salesforce reports, you're leaving pipeline on the table.
posts.cta: How fragmented is your GTM data model right now, honestly?

inspired_by.author: Dan Tyre
inspired_by.summary: Dan shares insights on sales ops evolution into revenue operations, focusing on tech stack optimization. He recommends tools for automating workflows between sales, marketing, and customer success. Includes a checklist for auditing your RevOps maturity.

remixes.label: autopsy-report
remixes.hook: I just audited a company's GTM stack. They had three teams building reports from the same CRM. None of them talked to each other.
remixes.body:
I just audited a company's GTM stack. They had three teams building reports from the same CRM. None of them talked to each other.

Sales ops owned pipeline dashboards. Marketing ops owned attribution. CS ops owned renewal forecasting. All three pulled from Salesforce. All three had different definitions of "qualified."

The result? A board deck where marketing claimed 40% of revenue, sales claimed 80%, and CS had a completely separate number for expansion. The math didn't work because the taxonomy didn't work.

This is what happens when you let ops functions grow in silos. Each team optimizes their slice. Nobody owns the full picture.

RevOps isn't a department name change. It's the decision to unify the data model. One definition of a qualified account. One lifecycle stage taxonomy. One attribution framework that finance actually trusts.

The companies I see doing this well didn't start with a reorg. They started with a shared data dictionary. Literally a spreadsheet where sales, marketing, and CS agreed on what words mean.

That's the unsexy foundation. But it's the thing that makes everything downstream actually work.
remixes.cta: When's the last time your sales and marketing teams agreed on a definition of 'qualified'?

remixes.label: tactical-breakdown
remixes.hook: The fastest way to tell if a company has real RevOps or just renamed sales ops: ask who owns the lifecycle stages.
remixes.body:
The fastest way to tell if a company has real RevOps or just renamed sales ops: ask who owns the lifecycle stages.

If the answer is "sales ops manages our Salesforce stages," you're looking at a title change. If the answer is "our RevOps team manages a unified lifecycle from first touch through renewal," that's structural integration.

Here's the practical difference. In a sales ops model, a lead gets handed from marketing to sales at MQL. What happens after that handoff is sales ops territory. Marketing has no idea if their leads converted. CS has no idea what was promised in the sales cycle.

In a RevOps model, there's one connected data model. Marketing sees which content influenced closed deals. Sales sees which accounts are showing intent signals before outbound. CS sees the original ICP fit score and deal notes before the first onboarding call.

I've helped rebuild this at multiple companies. The technical work isn't hard. APIs, enrichment, a few custom objects. The hard part is getting three teams to agree on shared definitions and shared accountability.

That's not a technology problem. It's a politics problem. And it's the real reason RevOps roles exist.
remixes.cta: Who in your org has a single view across marketing, sales, and CS data right now?

posts.title: career pivots look obvious in hindsight
posts.hook: Three years ago I was an SDR manually building buying committees in Salesforce. Now I build the automation systems that replace that workflow.
posts.body:
Three years ago I was an SDR manually building buying committees in Salesforce. Now I build the automation systems that replace that workflow.

The pivot looks clean in a LinkedIn bio. It wasn't.

There was a 6-month stretch where I was writing Python scripts at night after full days of cold calling. Teaching myself APIs by building scrapers that pulled the same data I was manually copying from ZoomInfo. Nobody told me to do it. I just got tired of the copy-paste.

The first script I wrote saved me maybe 20 minutes a day. The second one saved an hour. By the third month I had a pipeline that did in 10 minutes what took me half a day manually.

My manager didn't care about the scripts. He cared that my pipeline numbers went up 40% while my peers' stayed flat.

That's the thing about career pivots. You don't announce them. You just start doing the new thing inside the old thing until the gap between what your title says and what you actually do gets too wide to ignore.

If you're an SDR or AE reading this and you're curious about the technical side... start building. The worst case is you get better at your current job. The best case is you build a new one.
posts.cta: What skill are you quietly building outside your job description right now?

inspired_by.author: Sahil Lavingia
inspired_by.summary: Sahil reflects on pivoting from a high-flying VC-backed startup to building Gumroad as a solo founder initially, focusing on shipping a simple product for creators. He discusses career pivot lessons from tech layoffs and the freedom of bootstrapping in public. His story motivates mid-career switches into indie building.

remixes.label: reverse-engineering-the-path
remixes.hook: Nobody plans to become a GTM engineer. You just automate enough of your own job that you accidentally become one.
remixes.body:
Nobody plans to become a GTM engineer. You just automate enough of your own job that you accidentally become one.

It started with a spreadsheet. I was an SDR spending two hours a day copying contact data from ZoomInfo into Salesforce. Building buying committees manually. Cross-referencing LinkedIn profiles to figure out reporting structures.

So I wrote a script. Nothing fancy. Just a Python scraper that pulled the same data I was manually copying and formatted it into something I could bulk upload.

That script saved me 10 hours a week. My manager noticed. Not because I told him. Because my pipeline numbers jumped while my activity metrics stayed flat.

Then I automated the next bottleneck. And the next one. Each script was ugly. Each one barely worked. But each one compounded.

Within a year I wasn't really an SDR anymore. I was spending more time building tools than making calls. The job title hadn't changed, but the actual work had shifted completely.

The career pivot everyone asks about wasn't a pivot. It was a series of small automations that gradually replaced the manual work until the manual work was gone and only the automation work was left.

That's the pattern I see in every GTM engineer I know. Nobody went to school for this. They just got tired of doing the same thing twice.
remixes.cta: What repetitive task in your current role are you one script away from automating?

remixes.label: honest-mess
remixes.hook: The messy middle of a career pivot is the part nobody posts about. Here's what mine actually looked like.
remixes.body:
The messy middle of a career pivot is the part nobody posts about. Here's what mine actually looked like.

Month 1-2: Decided I wanted to learn Python. Watched YouTube tutorials after work. Understood maybe 30% of what was happening. Kept going.

Month 3-4: Built my first real script. A scraper that pulled data I was manually copying every day as an SDR. It broke constantly. I fixed it at midnight because I didn't want to admit at work that I was automating my own job.

Month 5-6: Started automating more of my workflow. My numbers went up. Nobody asked how. I didn't volunteer the information. I was genuinely worried they'd tell me to stop.

Month 7-9: Built something for a colleague. Then another. People started calling me "the scripts guy." Still had the SDR title. Still making cold calls during the day.

Month 10-12: Got pulled into a RevOps project because someone told the VP I could "do stuff with data." First time my automation work was officially part of my job.

The LinkedIn version of this story is two sentences. The lived version was a year of parallel lives. Working the day job while building the skills for the next one.

Every career pivot has a messy middle. The people who make it through aren't more talented. They just kept building when it wasn't paying off yet.
remixes.cta: What's your messy middle right now? What are you building that hasn't paid off yet?

Career pivots rarely happen as clean transitions. What I've observed, both in my own path and in the people I mentor, is that they happen through skill stacking on the margins.

The first script I wrote saved me maybe 20 minutes a day. The second one saved an hour. By the third month I had a pipeline that did in 10 minutes what took me half a day manually.

My manager didn't care about the scripts. He cared that my pipeline numbers went up 40% while my peers' stayed flat.

That's the thing about career pivots. You don't announce them. You just start doing the new thing inside the old thing until the gap between what your title says and what you actually do gets too wide to ignore.

If you're an SDR or AE reading this and you're curious about the technical side... start building. The worst case is you get better at your current job. The best case is you build a new one.

posts.title: shipping MVPs with AI is a trap
posts.hook: AI makes it easy to ship an MVP in a weekend. It also makes it easy to ship something nobody wants in a weekend.
posts.body:
AI makes it easy to ship an MVP in a weekend. It also makes it easy to ship something nobody wants in a weekend.

The speed increase is real. I've gone from idea to deployed Next.js app in a single afternoon. Claude Code handles the scaffolding, the component structure, the API routes. What used to take a week of setup takes hours.

But speed amplifies whatever direction you're pointed in. If you're pointed at a real problem, you get to validation faster. If you're pointed at a vanity project, you just waste a weekend faster.

I've shipped things that got traction on day one because the problem was obvious and validated before I wrote a line of code. I've also shipped things that got zero users because I skipped the uncomfortable part... actually talking to the people I was building for.

The builders who win right now aren't the fastest shippers. They're the ones who spend 80% of their time on problem selection and 20% on building. AI compresses the 20%. Nothing compresses the 80%.

Taste scales. Speed without taste just produces more noise.
posts.cta: What's the fastest you've killed something you built after realizing the problem wasn't real?

remixes.label: speed-trap
remixes.hook: The most dangerous developer tool isn't one that slows you down. It's one that makes you fast before you're ready.
remixes.body:
The most dangerous developer tool isn't one that slows you down. It's one that makes you fast before you're ready.

I shipped three projects in January using Claude Code. Full Next.js apps. Deployed to Vercel. Working products in a weekend each.

Two of them are dead now. Not because the code was bad. The code was fine. They died because I skipped the part where you figure out if anyone actually wants what you're building.

AI tools collapse the build cycle from weeks to hours. That's genuinely transformative. But they don't collapse the validation cycle. Talking to users still takes the same amount of time. Understanding a market still takes research. Finding distribution still requires strategy.

What I've noticed in my own work is that speed creates a specific type of bias. When building is cheap, you stop questioning whether you should build. Every problem looks solvable with a weekend project. Every idea feels worth prototyping.

The discipline I've had to develop is the opposite of what AI tools encourage. Slow down before you start. Spend the first day on the problem, not the solution. Talk to three people who have the problem before you write a line of code.

The weekend MVP is still valuable. But only after the week of validation that should come before it.
remixes.cta: How many of your last five projects solved a problem you validated before building?

remixes.label: contrarian-flip
remixes.hook: Hot take: the best thing AI can do for your product development process is help you kill ideas faster.
remixes.body:
Hot take: the best thing AI can do for your product development process is help you kill ideas faster.

Everyone talks about AI-assisted building. Ship an MVP in a weekend. Go from idea to deployed app in a day. And that's real. I do it regularly.

But the actual superpower is using that speed for rapid invalidation instead of rapid construction.

Here's what I mean. Before AI tools, killing an idea required either building it (expensive) or guessing it wouldn't work (unreliable). Now you can build a functional prototype in hours, put it in front of real users, and get signal the same day.

The shift isn't "build more things faster." It's "test more hypotheses faster." Those are completely different operating modes.

In the first mode, you end up with a graveyard of deployed apps nobody uses. I have that graveyard. I've learned from it.

In the second mode, you use the speed to run cheap experiments. Build the smallest possible version. Show it to five people. If nobody cares, kill it Monday morning and start the next experiment.

The teams I see using AI well aren't shipping more products. They're killing bad ideas in days instead of months. The ones using it poorly are just producing more stuff nobody asked for, but producing it really efficiently.
remixes.cta: What's the last idea you killed quickly instead of building out? How did you know?

posts.title: content strategy for RevOps teams
posts.hook: Your RevOps team is sitting on the best content engine in the company and doesn't know it.
posts.body:
Your RevOps team is sitting on the best content engine in the company and doesn't know it.

They see the conversion data. They know which segments close fastest. They know which objections kill deals. They know what the top 10% of reps say differently in discovery calls.

That's not operational data. That's content strategy.

I build content pipelines that pull from CRM signals, enrichment data, and win/loss analysis. The posts that perform best aren't the ones where I share opinions. They're the ones where I share patterns from real data.

"Deals with 3+ stakeholders mapped before first demo close 2.4x faster." That's a data point from our pipeline. It's also a LinkedIn post. It's also an email subject line. It's also a slide in a sales deck.

Most companies have marketing writing content in a vacuum and ops hoarding data in dashboards nobody reads. The gap between those two functions is where pipeline goes to die.

If your RevOps team isn't feeding patterns back to content, you're leaving organic pipeline on the table. The data already exists. Someone just has to translate it into something a buyer would actually read.
posts.cta: Does your content team have access to your CRM data? If not, why not?

remixes.label: hidden-goldmine
remixes.hook: Your CRM has better content ideas than your content team's brainstorm doc. Here's why nobody's using them.
remixes.body:
Your CRM has better content ideas than your content team's brainstorm doc. Here's why nobody's using them.

Last month I pulled closed-won deal data for a client and mapped it against their blog calendar. The disconnect was staggering. Their content team was writing thought leadership about industry trends. Their fastest-closing deals all came from prospects who searched for very specific, tactical problems.

The content team didn't know this because they didn't have CRM access. They were brainstorming topics from industry reports and competitor blogs. Meanwhile, the sales team was sitting on a goldmine of validated content topics and nobody was extracting them.

Here's the pipeline I built. Gong call transcripts get summarized weekly. Top objections, questions, and pain points get extracted. Those get cross-referenced with which deal stages they appear in. The output is a prioritized content brief queue ranked by revenue impact.

The first piece of content from this pipeline outperformed their previous quarter's top post by 3x in qualified traffic. Not because it was better written. Because it addressed the exact question prospects were asking in discovery calls.

Content strategy without CRM data is guessing. You might guess well sometimes. But you're leaving a structured signal source completely untapped.
remixes.cta: What's the most common question your sales team hears in discovery? Is your content team writing about it?

remixes.label: systems-approach
remixes.hook: I built a content pipeline that reads CRM signals. It generates better topic ideas than most content strategists I've worked with.
remixes.body:
I built a content pipeline that reads CRM signals. It generates better topic ideas than most content strategists I've worked with.

Not because AI is smarter than people. Because it has access to data that content teams typically don't see.

The system works like this. Enrichment data and deal stage progression feed into a scoring model. Which industries close fastest? Which personas convert at the highest rate? Which objections show up right before deals die?

Those signals become content briefs. Not vague topic suggestions. Specific angles tied to specific segments with specific proof points from actual deal data.

For example: one client's data showed that mid-market fintech companies were closing 2x faster than enterprise. The content team was writing exclusively for enterprise personas. We shifted 40% of their calendar to mid-market fintech pain points. Pipeline from content doubled in one quarter.

The second layer is competitive. The system tracks which keywords competitors rank for, maps them against our deal data, and identifies gaps where we have customer proof but no content.

Most content strategies are built on intuition and SEO tools. Both are fine inputs. But they miss the most valuable signal in the building: what your actual buyers actually care about, as measured by what they do with their wallets.
remixes.cta: How much of your content calendar is informed by actual deal data versus keyword research?

posts.title: vercel + AI agents changed my deploy workflow
posts.hook: My deployment pipeline has an AI agent in it. Not as a gimmick. Because it catches things I miss.
posts.body:
My deployment pipeline has an AI agent in it. Not as a gimmick. Because it catches things I miss.

Before every push to production, a pre-push hook scans for sensitive data. Client names, API keys, partner identifiers. I've been burned before. Deployed to the wrong Vercel project once. Accidentally committed a .env once. Both times cost me hours of cleanup.

So I automated the paranoia.

The pipeline: Claude Code runs a blocklist scan on all staged files. Checks commit messages for anything identifiable. Verifies the correct Vercel project is targeted. Then builds, commits, pushes, and verifies the deployment landed.

The whole thing runs in under 2 minutes. The scan adds maybe 15 seconds. That 15 seconds has caught 3 near-misses in the last month alone.

This is what AI in developer workflows actually looks like. Not "AI writes my app." More like "AI is the safety net between my local branch and production." The boring, unglamorous, absolutely critical layer that prevents the 2am incident.

Vercel's platform makes the deploy side trivial. The agent layer makes the safety side automatic. Together they turned my solo deploy workflow into something more reliable than teams I've worked on with dedicated DevOps.
posts.cta: What's in your pre-deploy safety checklist?

remixes.label: paranoia-driven-development
remixes.hook: I committed a .env file to a public repo once. That one mistake shaped my entire deployment philosophy.
remixes.body:
I committed a .env file to a public repo once. That one mistake shaped my entire deployment philosophy.

It was 11 PM. I was rushing to push a fix. Didn't check git status. The .env had API keys, database credentials, a Stripe secret key. By the time I noticed, the commit was on GitHub for 20 minutes.

I rotated every key that night. Took three hours. Some of those services didn't have easy key rotation. One required a support ticket that took two days to resolve.

That experience taught me something: the best safety systems aren't the ones you remember to run. They're the ones that run themselves.

So I built automated paranoia into my pipeline. A pre-push hook that scans every staged file for patterns: API key formats, client names from a local blocklist, .env patterns, anything that looks like a secret. If it finds something, the push fails. No override. No "are you sure?" prompt. Just a hard stop.

The hook has caught real issues four times since I built it. Twice it flagged client names in code comments. Once it caught a hardcoded API key in a test file. Once it blocked a config file I'd accidentally staged.

Four potential incidents, zero actual incidents. That's the math that matters.

I also added a Vercel project verification step after deploying to the wrong project once. The system confirms the project name before every deploy. Another five seconds that prevents another potential disaster.
remixes.cta: What's the worst thing you've accidentally committed? What did you build to prevent it from happening again?

remixes.label: practical-blueprint
remixes.hook: Here's the exact pre-deploy safety system I run before every push. It's saved me at least four times.
remixes.body:
Here's the exact pre-deploy safety system I run before every push. It's saved me at least four times.

Step one: pre-push Git hook. Scans all staged files against a local blocklist. Client names, API key patterns, .env file contents, partner identifiers. If anything matches, the push fails with a specific error telling you what it found and where.

Step two: Vercel project verification. After deploying to the wrong project once (shipped a client's staging config to my personal site), I added a check that confirms the target project name before every deploy. Takes five seconds. Prevents a specific class of mistake I've already made.

Step three: AI-assisted code review. Before the push hook runs, a lightweight scan checks for patterns a regex might miss. Hardcoded URLs with internal domains. Comments that reference specific clients. Variable names that leak context about who the project is for.

The whole pipeline adds maybe 30 seconds to each push. I don't notice it anymore. But it's caught four real issues since I set it up.

The philosophy: if you've made a mistake once, automate the prevention. Don't rely on being more careful next time. You won't be. Build systems that assume you'll be careless, because eventually you will be.

Every safety check in my pipeline exists because I made the specific mistake it prevents. That's not good engineering. It's scar tissue. But scar tissue works.
remixes.cta: What's one deployment mistake you've made that you haven't automated prevention for yet?

Let me walk you through the deployment safety system I recommend for any solo developer or small team shipping to production regularly.

The principle is simple: never rely on human memory for security checks. Automate every safety gate.

First, implement a pre-push Git hook. This should scan staged files for sensitive patterns: API keys, credentials, .env contents, and any client or partner identifiers you maintain in a local blocklist. If it finds a match, the push should fail with a clear error. No override option.

Third, consider adding an AI-assisted scan layer. Regex catches known patterns, but AI can flag contextual issues: hardcoded internal URLs, client references in comments, or variable names that leak project context.

The total overhead is about 30 seconds per push. The ROI is avoiding the 3-hour incident response when something sensitive ships to production.

Start with the pre-push hook. That alone will catch 80% of accidental exposures. Layer the other checks as you encounter new failure modes.
